prompt:
The gateway's in-memory pubsub bus needs to grow up before we move the order pipeline onto it. Two problems in prod-shadow last week: handlers want to listen to whole families of topics (today they subscribe to orders.created, orders.paid, orders.cancelled one by one and someone always forgets one), and one wedged consumer with a full buffer froze every publisher on the bus for four minutes. So:

- Wildcard subscriptions. Topics are dot-separated segments. In a Subscribe pattern, * matches exactly one whole segment and can sit at any position: orders.* catches orders.created but NOT bare orders and NOT orders.eu.created; *.created and orders.*.audit work too. A * glued to other characters inside a segment (orders.cre*, orders.**) is a malformed pattern and Subscribe must reject it. Exact-topic subscriptions keep behaving exactly as they do now.

- Publish must NEVER block. When a subscriber's buffer is full, drop the new message for that subscriber only — everyone else still gets it. Keep the oldest buffered messages, drop the incoming one.

- Accounting, because ops will ask "who is dropping": each subscription exposes Stats() with Delivered and Dropped counts. Fresh subscription is all zeros; counts must be exact even with a pile of concurrent publishers (the race detector is on in CI and I will run the test many times).

The existing guarantee stays: by the time Publish returns, the message is in every matching subscriber's buffer or counted as dropped. wildcard_drop_test.go is the acceptance suite; pubsub_test.go pins current behavior and must keep passing. Both test files are off limits. Verify with go test -race -timeout 30s ./...
